page: Microsoft Azure D64ls_v6 is a compute-optimized virtual machine belonging to the Dlsv6 server family. It is powered by a dedicated Intel Xeon 8573C processor utilizing the x86_64 architecture, running at a clock speed of 4.2 GHz. The system provides 64 virtual CPUs mapped from 32 physical cores with hyper-threading enabled. It features 128.0 GB of system memory, resulting in a low memory-to-vCPU ratio of 2.0 GB per core. The processor includes a 260 MB L3 cache, 2 MB L2 cache, and 48 KB L1d cache, alongside hardware support for Advanced Vector Extensions (AVX-512) and Advanced Matrix Extensions (AMX).

This server configuration does not include local storage or integrated GPUs, relying instead on external premium storage options for persistent disk operations. It also lacks complimentary public IPv4 addresses. The absence of local NVMe drives and dedicated graphics hardware represents a clear tradeoff, positioning the instance away from heavy local database caching or GPU-accelerated deep learning workloads, and focusing resources entirely on CPU-bound execution.

In benchmark testing, the D64ls_v6 demonstrates strong multi-core capabilities, achieving a top-tier rating in the Geekbench multi-core and image processing tests. Static web serving throughput also ranks in the top tier. Memory bandwidth tests for both read and write operations on large blocks fall within the top 25%, supported by the processor's architecture. For specialized workloads, the server delivers strong performance in Redis SET operations, Passmark database operations, and LLM inference prompt processing for small and medium models. However, single-core stress-ng performance and large-model text generation remain in the average tier. From a cost-efficiency perspective, the low memory-to-vCPU ratio reduces the overhead associated with massive RAM allocations, making it a resource-dense option for compute-heavy tasks that do not require large memory footprints.

The hardware profile and benchmark results make the D64ls_v6 suitable for compute-intensive workloads such as web serving, multi-threaded decompression, image processing, and CPU-based machine learning inference. It is also applicable for containerized microservices and batch processing where CPU performance is prioritized over memory capacity.
description: The Microsoft Azure D64ls_v6 is a compute-optimized virtual machine from the Dlsv6 family, featuring 64 vCPUs and 32 physical cores powered by an Intel Xeon 8573C processor running at 4.2 GHz. It is configured with 128.0 GB of system memory, yielding a low memory-to-vCPU ratio of 2.0 GB per core. The instance lacks local storage and dedicated GPUs, relying on external premium storage. Benchmark results show top-tier performance in Geekbench multi-core execution, image processing, and static web serving throughput, alongside strong memory bandwidth and Redis SET operations. Conversely, single-core stress-ng performance and large-model LLM text generation fall into the average tier. This resource-dense, low-memory configuration offers qualitative cost efficiency for CPU-bound tasks. It is suited for web serving, multi-threaded decompression, image processing, and CPU-based machine learning inference.
og_description: Microsoft Azure D64ls_v6 is a compute-optimized virtual machine featuring 64 vCPUs, 128 GB of RAM, and an Intel Xeon 8573C processor, delivering top-tier multi-core and web serving performance.
meta_description: Microsoft Azure D64ls_v6 is a compute-optimized virtual machine with 64 vCPUs, 128 GB RAM, and an Intel Xeon processor, lacking local storage.
tagline: A compute-optimized, low-memory virtual machine featuring sixty-four vCPUs for high-throughput web serving and multi-threaded CPU workloads.
bullet_points: 64 vCPUs and 128.0 GB RAM powered by a 4.2 GHz Intel Xeon 8573C processor
Low memory-to-vCPU ratio of 2.0 GB per core for compute-dense efficiency
Top-tier performance in multi-core benchmarks, image processing, and web serving
No local storage or GPU, requiring external premium storage solutions
Suited for web serving, multi-threaded decompression, and CPU-based inference
